summary: Joel Spolsky argues that simplicity in software is overrated and often misunderstood as a product strategy. He contends that the 80/20 rule fails because everyone uses a different 20% of features, making 'lite' products frustrating for most users. While he acknowledges simplicity works as a bootstrapping strategy for resource-constrained startups, he argues it fails as a long-term approach because competitors can easily clone simple products and users ultimately want more features. He distinguishes between simplicity as usability or aesthetic minimalism (both valid) versus simplicity as deliberately lacking features (a losing strategy), citing his own experience at Fog Creek where new feature releases consistently drove the most revenue growth.
key_points: The 80/20 rule for features fails because every user relies on a different 20%, so any 'lite' product will frustrate most of its potential audience
Simple products work as bootstrapping strategies for resource-constrained startups, but this is a happy coincidence of necessity rather than a proven long-term approach
Products like the iPod and 37signals succeed not because of simplicity per se, but because of beauty, usability, emotional appeal, and design — which are themselves features
At Fog Creek, nothing increased revenue more than releasing new versions with more features — not marketing, not press coverage, not advertising
Simplicity as 'easy to use' or 'clean aesthetic' is valid; simplicity as 'deliberately fewer features' is a misattribution of what makes products successful
money_quotes: 80% of the people use 20% of the features. So you convince yourself that you only need to implement 20% of the features, and you can still sell 80% as many copies. Unfortunately, it's never the same 20%.
So you sell 'simple' as if it were this wonderful thing, when, coincidentally, it's the only thing you have the resources to produce.

I think it is a misattribution to say, for example, that the iPod is successful because it lacks features. If you start to believe that, you'll believe, among other things, that you should take out features to increase your product's success.
Nothing we have ever done at Fog Creek has increased our revenue more than releasing a new version with more features. Nothing.
The Hummer is aesthetically appealing precisely because it's ugly and complicated.
The flow to our bottom line from new versions with new features is absolutely undeniable. It's like gravity.

If you think simplicity means 'not very many features' or 'does one thing and does it well,' then I applaud your integrity but you can't go that far with a product that deliberately leaves features out.
key_insight: Simplicity is often confused with usability and good design, but deliberately reducing features is not what makes products successful — users want all the features, they just want them to be well-designed and easy to use.
